Q9_evidence: Figure 4: AIG New Zealand's Climate Change Scenarios — Orderly Scenario: An ambitious and coordinated transition aligned with a 1.5˚C warming trajectory; Disorderly Scenario: Delayed action, followed by sudden and uncoordinated transformation, landing at <2˚C; Hot House World: Continuation of current policy settings, leading to uncontrolled warming of 3˚C+ by 2100
Q10_answer: Three temperature outcomes are described: (1) 1.5°C warming trajectory (Orderly), with medium to high carbon removal dialling back to 1.4°C by end-century; (2) below 2.0°C this century (Disorderly); (3) 3°C+ uncontrolled warming by 2100 (Hot House World).
Q10_evidence: Orderly: 'International and domestic policy settings aim to limit total warming by end-of-century to less than 1.5˚C... Medium to high deployment of carbon removal solutions dials the heat back to 1.4˚C warming by end-century.' Disorderly: 'By 2035, New Zealand's Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) to the Paris Agreement, in combination with other countries around the world, are sufficient to keep total warming below 2.0˚C this century.' Hot House World: 'New Zealand's Nationally Determined Contributions to the Paris Agreement, in combination with other countries around the world, are insufficient to keep total warming below 3.0˚C this century.'
Q11_answer: The Insurance Council of New Zealand (ICNZ) is the primary scenario provider. The narratives draw heavily on the Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) framework. Technical experts from the External Reporting Board (XRB), government ministries, banking partners, and the New Zealand Green Building Council contributed to ICNZ's shared climate change scenarios.

NZ 1.5°C Transition – aligned with limiting global warming to 1.5°C.

CLIMADA was selected for its open-source accessibility and its ability to model climate hazard impacts at asset level.

Q21_evidence: Green light 2.6: RCP2.6 is the most stringent mitigation scenario in which carbon dioxide emissions decline to net zero relatively quickly. It reflects a world in which warming is limited to around 1.5-2°C by 2100. SSP1: Sustainability reflects a world in which energy affordability and human well-being is prioritised. There are 'low challenges to mitigation and adaptation'. This aligned well with the rapid and smooth transition described in Green Light. Amber light 4.5: RCP4.5 illustrates global emissions peak around 2040 and slowly begin to decline thereafter. Similar climatic impacts are expected in the disorderly scenario described in this report. This reflects a world where global warming reaches 2.6°C by 2100. SSP2: Middle of the Road describes a world with largely similar socio-economic trends of today with 'medium challenges to mitigation and adaptation'. This aligns well with the lack of action until the mid-2030s, when dramatic changes are enforced. Red light 7.0: RCP7.0 presents a trajectory of over 3.5°C global warming by 2100. This scenario features growing emissions, leading to severe physical impacts and is understood to be the worst-case of climate scenarios. SSP3: Regional rivalry describes a world with material focused consumption and low international priority for addressing environmental concerns. This aligns well with the lack of political action and technological development over time.

Q9_answer: ['Net Zero Revolution', 'Adaptive Evolution', 'Hot House']
Q9_evidence: Meridian climate scenarios overview: Net Zero Revolution – Emissions are in line with the Paris Agreement climate goal of limiting global warming to 1.5°C by the end of the century, with low overshoots (<0.1°C) of 1.5°C in earlier years. This leads to high transition risks, but lower physical climate risks. Adaptive Evolution – There is a lack of international coordination on the climate response, with regions operating independently. Emissions decline but lead nonetheless to 2.7°C of warming by 2100, associated with moderate to severe physical risks. Transition risks are low-moderate. Hot House – Globally, only currently implemented policies are preserved. Emissions increase until 2100, leading to 3°C+ of warming by 2100 and severe physical risks but lower transition risks.
